Facts.text: Comet 1P/374 E1 (Halley) approaches within 0.0884 AU of Earth
Greek pope John VII chosen as successor to John VI
Maya city of Piedras Negras celebrates military triumph over nearby settlement of Pomona (as depicted in Stela 12 Piedras Negras)
The body of the first bishop of Tournai, Saint Eleutherius of Tournai, is translated (moved)
Berwick-upon-Tweed is captured by the Scots from the English
Danish squire and national hero Niels Ebbesen kills German prince Gerhard III of Holstein in his bedroom, ending the 1332 to 1340 interregnum in Denmark
English guilds/corporations go under state control
Watergeuzen capture Brielle from the Spaniards, during the Eighty Years' War, gaining the first foothold on land for what would become the Dutch Republic.
Gemert in the Netherlands fines unwed mothers a penalty of 50 guilders
Cotton Mather's four-day-old son dies, and witchcraft is blamed
Friedrich von Klinger's "Sturm und Drang" premieres in Leipzig
New Orleans businessman Oliver Pollock creates the "$" symbol
Gronings feminist Etta Palm demands women's right to divorce
French law rules the use of intention
Italian composer Luigi Cherubini becomes director of the Conservatoire de Paris music college, holds position for nearly 20 years
San Francisco County government established
Cincinnati becomes 1st US city to employ fulltime professional firefighters
Shenandoah Valley campaign, Jackson's Battle of Woodstock, Virginia
1st wartime conscription law in US goes into effect
-9] Battle at Blakely Alabama
US Congress rejects presidential veto giving all equal rights in US
Blacks vote in municipal election in Tuscumbia, Alabama
International Exhibition opens in Paris
Singapore, Penang & Malacca become British crown colonies
Hampton Institute opens
New Constitution adopted by the German Empire
1st edition of The Standard
British White Star steamship Atlantic sinks off Nova Scotia, 547 die
Mehmed Kemals play "Vatan" premeres in Constantinople
Anti-Jewish riots in Jerusalem
Kingdom post office in Netherlands opens
Students from Rotterdam, Netherlands establish a cricket club called Rotterdamsche Cricket & Football Club Sparta which evolves into Sparta Rotterdam
1st dishwashing machine marketed (Chicago)
London-Paris telephone connection opens
The Wrigley Company is founded in Chicago, Illinois.
NC Mutual opens doors for business
1st edition of Dutch newspaper "The People"
"SOS" first adopted as a morse distress signal (· · · – – – · · ·) by German government
British East African Protectorate becomes colony of Kenya
Dumitru Dan (Romania) completes a 62,137 mile (100,000 m) walk
United Kingdom: the Royal Air Force is created from the Royal Naval Air Service and the Royal Flying Corps.
Stanley Cup Final, Seattle Ice Arena, Seattle, WA: With Montreal Canadiens (NHL) & Seattle Metropolitans (PCHA) tied at 2-2-1, trophy not awarded due to worldwide flu epidemic
The influential Bauhaus School opens in Weimar, Germany, headed by architect Walter Gropius teaching painting, sculpture, architecture, and design
Stanley Cup Final, Mutual Street Arena, Toronto, ON: Jack Darragh scores a hat-trick as Ottawa Senators (NHL) beat Seattle Metropolitans (PCHA), 6-1 for a 3-2 series win
The (Anglican) Church in Wales disestablished
Crown takes over Northern Rhodesia from British South Africa Co
Imperial Airways forms in Britain
The Royal Canadian Air Force is formed.
1st transmission of Danish state radio
Hebrew University, Jerusalem dedicated [see May 9, 1925]
Montreal Maroons' Clint Benedict becomes first NHL goalie to record 3 straight playoff shutouts, in a 3-0 win against the visiting Victoria Cougars in Game 2 of the Stanley Cup Championship series
1st automatic record changer introduced by His Master's Voice
Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist army crosses the Yangtze River
Austrian government of Ignaz Seipel falls
Doorne's trailer factory in Eindhoven Netherlands opens
Louie Marx introduces Yo-Yo
Luis Buñuel releases "Un Chien Andalou" 24-minute film
Morehouse College, Spellman College & Atlanta University affiliate
Chicago Cubs catcher Leo Hartnett breaks the altitude record for a catch by gloving a baseball dropped from the Goodyear blimp 800 feet over Los Angeles, California
Earthquake devastate Managua Nicaragua, kills 2,000
Jackie Mitchell becomes the second female (after Lizzie Arlington 1898) in organised baseball when she signs with the Chattanooga Lookouts Baseball Club
England cricket batsman Wally Hammond smashes 34 fours, 10 sixes in a Test record unbeaten 336 in the drawn 2nd Test against New Zealand in Auckland
Nazi Germany begins persecution of Jews by boycotting Jewish businesses
1st radio tube made of metal announced, Schenectady, NY
Orissa constituted a province of British India
Aden becomes a British crown colony
Nescafé introduces their flagship brand in Switzerland
Nazis forbid Jewish access to cafés
Pro-German Rashid Ali al-Ghailani grabs power in Iraq
The Blockade Runner Badge for German Kriegsmarine is instituted.
US Navy takes over Treasure Island in San Francisco Bay
Allied air raid on Japanese occupied harbor city Kupang, Timor, Indonesia
Mexico changes from 3 time zones to 2
Jan Dieters, Dutch politician, resistance fighter, and a leading member of Communist Party of the Netherlands, is arrested by the Nazis
Japanese troops conquer Jessami, Eastern India
World War II: Canadian troops free the Dutch cities of Doetinchem, Enschede, Borculo and Eibergen
World War II: The Ruhr Pocket of German forces are encircled by the US Ninth Army and US First Army, eventually leading to the capture of 317,000 German troops
400,000 US mine workers strike
The Malayan Union is formed as a federation of the Malay states and the Straits Settlements of Penang and Malacca
Tsunamis generated by a quake in Aleutian Trench strike Hilo, Hawaii
Van Acker forms Belgian government (without CVP)
1st Jewish immigrants to Israel disembark at Port of Eilat
"Big Bang" theory proposed in scientific journal "Physical Review" by American cosmologists Ralph Alpher, Hans Bethe, and George Gamow
Faroe Islands receive autonomy from Denmark
Fast bowler Hines Johnson has match figures of 10-96 on debut in West Indies 4th cricket Test win v England in Kingston, Jamaica; first fast bowler to take 10 wickets in a single Test for West Indies
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
J van Bale appointed governor of New Guinea
KXMC TV channel 13 in Minot, ND (CBS/ABC) begins broadcasting
1st Dutch motorway, Amsterdam-Utrecht, opens
1st US Army helicopter battalion forms, Fort Bragg, North Carolina
Earthquake/tsunami ravage Aleutians, 200 killed
US Air Force Academy is established in Colorado Springs
WQED TV channel 13 in Pittsburgh, PA (PBS) begins broadcasting
Armed military action taken against bureaucratic strike in Amsterdam
EOKA-bomb attacks against British government buildings in Cyprus
WTVT TV channel 13 in Tampa-St Petersburg, FL (CBS) 1st broadcast
10th Tony Awards: "The Diary of Anne Frank" (play) & "Damn Yankees" (musical) win
KPIC TV channel 4 in Roseburg, OR (CBS) begins broadcasting
Violent clashes in Algeria, kills at least 380
Trial begins in Budapest against participants of the Hungarian Uprising of October 1956
World's biggest glass oven used
WYES-TV channel 12 in New Orleans, Louisiana (PBS) begins broadcasting
KVIQ TV channel 6 in Eureka, CA (NBC/ABC/CBS) begins broadcasting
Marshal Nikolai Bulganin appointed Chairman of the Board of the State Bank of the USSR
1st weather satellite launched (TIROS 1)
2nd French atom bomb explodes (Sahara)
Census determines the resident population of the United States to be 179,245,000
France performs nuclear test at Reggane Proving Grounds, Algeria
American television soap opera premieres: "General Hospital" (ABC) & "Doctors" (NBC)
NYC's newspapers resume publishing after a 114 day strike
10°F lowest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland in April
Military coup in Brazil by General Castello Branco ousts President João Goulart
Robert Lowell's "Benito Cereno" premieres in NYC
South Africa worker's union leader Henry Fazzie sentenced to 10 years
Syncom 3, 1st geosynchronous communications satellite, passes from civilian to military control
"Hold On, I'm Comin'" single released by Sam & Dave
1st world festival of black art (Dakar Senegal)
1st British ombudsman Sir Edward Compton begins work
The United States Department of Transportation begins operation.
KEMO (now KOFY) TV channel 20 in San Francisco, California (IND) 1st broadcast
Royal Canadian Mint formally forms as a Crown Corporation
Seattle Pilots trade little-known minor league outfielder Lou Piniella to KC Royals for 2 prospects; hits .282 with 11 HRs & 68 RBI to win AL Rookie of the Year; becomes leading MLB manager
The Hawker Siddeley Harrier (vertical take-off fighter) enters service with the RAF.
Federal Bankruptcy Referee Sidney Volinn declares MLB's Seattle Pilots bankrupt; car dealer Bud Selig buys franchise for $10.8 million and moves club to Milwaukee (Brewers)
John Lennonand Yoko Ono release hoax they are having dual sex change operations
Serious riots continue in the Ballymurphy estate in Belfast between Catholic residents and the British Army
United Kingdom lifts all restrictions on gold ownership
US/Canada ISIS 2 launched to study ionosphere
30,000 attend Mar Y Sol rock concert, Vega Baja, Puerto Rico
MLB players stage first ever collective strike; play resumes (13/4/72) when owners and players agree to $500,000 increase in pension fund payments
Japan allows its citizens to own gold
John Lennonand Yoko Ono form a new country with no laws or boundaries, called Nutopia, its national anthem is silence
OPEC increases price of petroleum by 5.7 percent
In England, "Local Government Act 1972" redraws the administrative map and creates six new Metropolitan and non-metropolitan counties; Newport and Monmouthshire transferred from England to Wales
Pioneer Hall opens at Disney’s Fort Wilderness Resort & Campground, Florida
Yourdon Inc consulting formed by Edward Yourdon
Cambodia President Lon Nol flees for Red Khmer
Conrail takes over operations from six bankrupt railroads in the northeastern U.S..
Jovian-Plutonian gravitational effect is first reported by the astronomer Patrick Moore
Attempt for Muslim state in Chad fails
Jean Shepherd, American humorist and storyteller, ends long-running radio program on WOR-AM in NYC, after more than 22 years
Philippine College of Commerce becomes the Polytechnic University of the Philippines by presidential decree
Iran proclaimed an Islamic Republic following fall of Shah
Warner Cable launches Nickelodeon, a basic cable channel featuring programming focused on children and teenagers
Failed assassination attempt on Iraqi vice-premier Tariq Aziz
France performs underground nuclear test at the Moruroa Atoll in French Polynesia
New York City's Transit Worker Union 100 begins a strike lasting 11 days
Daylight saving time is introduced in the USSR.
Anguilla (dependent territory of UK) adopts constitution
US formally transfers Canal Zone to Panama
Anti-nuclear demonstrators link arms in 14-mile human chain in England
Iraq increases missile attacks on Iran
14th Easter Seal Telethon raises $24,600,000
8 men record longest distance (13 miles) rowed in 24 hours
Ranji Trophy Men's Cricket, Feroz Shah Kotla, Delhi: Delhi beats Haryana by innings & 141; Manoj Prabhakar 113, Kirti Azad 107, Mohinder Amarnath 194, Ajay Sharma 110
US submarine Nathaniel Green runs aground in Irish Sea
World oil prices dip below $10 a barrel
"Ha!" comedy Channel on cable TV begins transmitting
It becomes illegal in Salem Oregon to be within 2 feet of nude dancers
Iran releases British hostage Roger Cooper after 5 years
Byzantine Emperor Justin I names his nephewJustinian Ias co-ruler and successor to the throne
Portuguese fleet underAfonso de Albuquerquecapture the Persian fortress of Hormuz, renaming it the Fort of Our Lady of the Conception
Henry Pelhambecomes English minister of War
Jonathan Swiftpublishes "Drapier's Letters" a series of seven pamphlets against a privately minted copper coinage Swift saw as inferior and corrupt
Samuel Moreyis issued the first U.S. patent for an internal-combustion engine, which he calls a “Gas or Vapour Engine"
"Hard Times" begins serialisation inCharles Dickensmagazine, "Household Words"
Herman Melvillepublishes the novel "The Confidence-Man: His Masquerade" on April Fool's Day in New York
US Civil War: Battle of Five Forks: Union Army captures crucial supply route point and 4,000 Confederate soldiers in Dinwiddie County, Virginia
French painterPaul Gauguinleaves Marseille for Tahiti
Henry Miller's Theater opens at 124 W 43rd St NYC
Adolf Hitlersentenced to 5 years labor for "Beer Hall Putsch" but General Ludendorff acquitted
"The Blue Angel" starringMarlene Dietrichin her breakthrough role premieres in Germany
American golferBobby Jonesstarts his Grand Slam season by winning the Southeastern Open by 13 strokes over Horton Smith at the Forest Hills-Ricker Golf Course in Atlanta, Georgia
Heinrich Himmlerbecomes Police Commander of Germany

Clyde Barrowkills two young highway patrolmen, H. D. Murphy and Edward Bryant Wheeler, at the intersection of Route 114 near Grapevine, Texas.Bonnie Parker's role in the murders helps turn public perception against the gang for good
Charles "Lucky" Luciano" is arrested in Arkansas on a criminal warrant from New York
World heavyweight boxing championJoe LouisKOs Harry Thomas in 5th round of their title bout in Chicago; 3rd defense by Louis
US recognizesFrancisco Franco's government in Spain at end of Spanish civil war. PopePius XIIcongratulates Generalissimo Franco's victory in Spain
Filipino PresidentQuezonofficially authorizes the printing and publication of the grammar and dictionary prepared by the Institute of the National Language.
Lillian Hellman's play "Watch on the Rhine" premieres in NYC
"Shin Takarajima" (New Treasure Island) byOsamu Tezukapublished, first manga cartoon in tankōbon form

Detroit Red Wings right wingGordie Howescores 2 goals and an assist, and sets a Stanley Cup playoff record for fastest goal from the start of a game (:09) in a 4-3 2OT win over Toronto in Game 5 of the Semi-Finals
U Nuelected premier of Burma for the third time
NY Mets purchase future Baseball Hall of Fame outfielderDuke Sniderfrom the LA Dodgers for $40,000; Snider had starred for the Brooklyn Dodgers 1947-57
John Lennonis reunited with his father Freddie 17 years after being abandoned at age 5

Loyalist led byIan Paisley, a Protestant fundamentalist preacher, founded the Ulster Constitution Defence Committee to challenge the civil rights movement; it set up a paramilitary-style wing called the Ulster Protestant Volunteers
US PresidentRichard Nixonsigns bill limiting cigarette advertisements from 1st Jan 1971

Boston Celtics captainJohn Havlicekscores career high 54 points in 134-109 rout of Atlanta Hawks in Game 1 of 1st round Eastern Conference playoffs; hits NBA playoff record 24-of-36 field-goal attempts
Despite a final round, 5-3 loss to Montreal, Boston Bruins centerPhil Espositowins his third consecutive NHL scoring title with 130 points from 55 goals and 75 assists in the 78-game season
Ayatollah Khomeinicalls for an Islamic Republic in Iran
"Helter Skelter", detailingCharles Manson's cult "family", their capture, trial, conviction and aftermath, premieres on CBS
Steve WozniakandSteve Jobsfound Apple Computer in the garage of Jobs' parents house in Cupertino, California
Last episode of "TheBob NewhartShow" sitcom (the Chicago one) airs on CBS-TV
New York Islanders right wingMike Bossybecomes first NHL rookie to score 50 goals in a season after scoring #50 and #51 in the closing moments in a 3-2 win over Washington
CNN airs a videotape that shows that Tamara Rand predicted thatReaganis in danger from someone named Jack Humley (a hoax)
Edmonton centerWayne Gretzkyhas an assist (his 103rd) in the Oilers 4-4 tie with Colorado Rockies to break Bobby Orr's 10-year mark for most assists in a single NHL season
Quebec centerPeter Šťastnýscores 4th career hat trick and 4 assists, and his brother Marián adds 5 assists to lead the Nordiques to an 8-5 win at Boston
A. Bartlett GiamattireplacesPeter Ueberrothas 7th commissioner of Major League Baseball; dies suddenly of a heart attack 5 months later
The ruins of Pompeii are rediscovered by Spaniard Roque Joaquín de Alcubierre
Battle of Okinawa: US ground forces invade Okinawa during World War II in the largest amphibious assault of the Pacific theatre
Go-Saga, 88th Emperor of Japan (1242-6), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1272)
Ludwig IV, Duke of Bavaria (1301-47), King of the Romans (1314-47) and Holy Roman Emperor (1328-47), born in Munich (d. 1347)
François de Bonne, Duke of Lesdiguières, last Constable of France to be appointed (1622-26), born in Saint-Bonnet-en-Champsaur (d. 1626)
William Harvey, English physician (discovered blood circulation), born in Folkestone, Kent (d. 1657)
Thomas Simpson, English composer who worked in Germany, born in Kent, England (d. 1628)
Charles de Saint-Évremond, French soldier who fled to exile in England, essayist and man of letters, born in Coutances, Normandy, France (d. 1703)
Guru Teg Bahadur Ji, Ninth of ten gurus who founded the Sikh religion and leader (1665-75), born in Amritsar, India (d. 1675)
Georg Mohr, Danish mathematician who proved the Mohr–Mascheroni theorem, born in Copenhagen. Denmark (d. 1697)
Christian Demelius, German composer and kapellmeister, born in Schlettau, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1711)
John Wilmot, 2nd Earl of Rochester, English poet (A Satyr Against Reason and Mankind) and courtier of King Charles II, born in Oxfordshire, England (d. 1680)
Abbé Prévost, French author and novelist (Manon Lescaut), born in Hesdin, Artois, France (d. 1763)
Pieter Hellendaal, Dutch violinist, organist (Pembroke College & Chapel of Peterhouse, both in Cambridge, England), teacher, and composer (Glees), born in Rotterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1799)
François Bainville, French organist and composer, born in France (d. 1788)
Joseph de Maistre, Savoyard diplomat, writer and a forefather of conservatism, born in Chambéry, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1821)
Jean Anthelme Brillat-Savarin, French lawyer and gastronome (wrote 1st book of gastronomy - The Physiology of Taste), born in Belley, France (d. 1826)[1]
Luigi Schiavonetti, Italian reproductive engraver and illustrator, born in Venetia, Italy (d. 1810)
Ignaz Franz von Mosel, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1844)
Ferdinand Olivier, German painter (Nazarene movement), born in Dessau (d. 1841)
Hubert Reis, German composer and violinist, born in Bonn, Electorate of Cologne, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1886)
Fredrik Cygnaeus, Finnish poet, literary critic and nationis, born in Hämeenlinna, Finland (d. 1881)
Willem Ruys, Dutch ship owner and founder of Rotterdam Lloyd (Nedlloyd), born in Ouderkerk aan de Amstel, Netherlands (d. 1889)
Simon Bolivar Buckner, American soldier, Confederate lieutenant general and 30th governor of Kentucky (1887-91), born in Munfordville, Kentucky (d. 1914)
Isidore Legouix, French composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1916)
Jim Fisk, American financier and "robber baron" of the Gilded Age known as Big Jim, born in Pownal, Vermont (d. 1872)
Ludwig Enneccerus, German politician and legal scholar, born in Neustadt am Rübenberge (d. 1928)
Jules-Nicolas Crevaux, French naval doctor and explorer (French Guiana and the Amazon), born in Lorquin, France (d. 1882)
Edward Austin Abbey, American painter (Quest of the Holy Grail mural at Boston Public Library), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1911)
Bill Traylor, African-American artist of folk and modern art, born near Pleasant Hill, Alabama (d. 1949)[1]
Acacio Gabriel Viegas, Indian physician who identified the plague in Bombay, born in Apora, Portuguese India (d. 1933)
Arnold Aletrino, Dutch physician, criminologist, and author (From Life), born in Amsterdam (d. 1916)
Gaetano Mosca, Italian sociologist (Circulation of Elite), born in Palermo, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (d. 1941)
Truby King, New Zealand physician, pioneer in infant welfare (Karitāne hospitals, Plunket Society), born near New Plymouth, Zealand (d. 1938)[1]
Marie Jungius, Dutch fairy tale writer, feminist and founder of the National Bureau for Women's Labor, born in Heiloo, Netherlands (d. 1908)
Richard Adolf Zsigmondy, Austrian-born chemist (1925 Nobel Prize for Chemistry for work colloids), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1929)[1]
Ferruccio Busoni, Italian piano prodigy, composer (Fantasia Contrappuntistica; Arlecchino), conductor, and pedagogue, born in Empoli, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1924)
Sophonisba Breckenridge, American professor, teacher and social activist (1st woman to graduate from Chicago Uni Law School), born in Lexington, Kentucky (d. 1948)[1]
Edmond Rostand, French poet and playwright (Cyrano de Bergerac), born in Marseille, France (d. 1918)
Peter Egge, Norwegian writer (De Droom), born in Trondheim, Norway (d. 1959)
F. Melius Christiansen, Norwegian-American violinist, and choral conductor (St. Olaf Choir, 1912-44), born in Eidsvold, Norway (d. 1955)
Tadeusz Joteyko, Polish conductor, composer, and musicologist (Zygmunt August), born in Poczujki, Russian Empire (d. 1932)
Sergei Rachmaninoff, Russian-American piano virtuoso, conductor, and composer (Aleko; Piano Concerto No. 3, Vocalise), born in Oneg or Semyonovo, Russian Empire (d. 1943) [OS=Mar 20]
Carl Sternheim, German playwright (Hyperion/Tabula Rasa), born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1942)
Agha Petros [Petros Elia of Baz], Assyrian military leader during World War I, born in Baz, Ottoman Empire (d. 1932)
Paul Anspach, Belgian fencer (Olympic gold individual epée, team epée 1912), born in Burcht, Belgium (d. 1981)
Alexander Vasilyevich Alexandrov, Russian composer (Alexnadrov Ensemble), born in Plakhino, Ryazan Governorate (d. 1946) [OS date]
Leonidas "Lon" Chaney, American actor nicknamed "the man of a 1000 faces" (The Hunchback Of Notre Dame, The Phantom of the Opera), born in Colorado Springs (d. 1930)

Hugo Bezdek, Czech American College Football Hall of Fame coach (University of Oregon, University of Arkansas, Penn State; NFL: Cleveland Browns; MLB: Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Prague, Czech Republic (d. 1952)
Laurette Taylor [Helen Loretta Cooney], American actress (Peg o' My Heart), born in New York City (d. 1946)
Clementine Churchill, British life peer and wife of Winston Churchill, born in London, England (d. 1977)
Casimir von Pászthory, Austrian composer, born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1966)
Leonard Bloomfield, American linguist (Structural Linguistics), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1949)
Frank Rankin, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame rover (Toronto Eatons, Toronto St. Michael's Majors) and coach (Toronto Granites-Canada Olympic gold 1924), born in Stratford, Ontario (d. 1932)
Cicely Courtneidge, Australian actress (Double Exposure), born in Sydney, New South Wales (d. 1980)
Alberta Hunter, American blues singer, and composer ("Downhearted Blues"), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1984)
Doc Alexander, American College Football Hall of Fame guard (Syracuse; All Pro 1921, 22; Rochester Jeffersons; NFL C'ship 1925; NY Giants), born in Silver Creek, New York (d. 1975)
Lucille Bogan, American blues singer and songwriter ("Sweet Petunia"; "Black Angel Blues"), born in Amory, Mississippi (d. 1948)
Pola Gojawiczynska, Polish writer (Stolica), born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1963)
Roger Bastide, French sociologist (study of Afro-Brazilian and Afro-Caribbean religions), born in Nimes, France (d. 1974)
William James Sidis, American mathematician and child prodigy, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1944)
Gustavs Celmins, Latvian politician, born in Riga, Russian Empire (d. 1968)[1]
Lida Durdikova, Czech children's author (Children with extinguished eyes) and publisher (Père Castor), born in Prague (d. 1955)
Stefanie Clausen, Danish diver (Olympic gold 10m platform 1920), born in Vesterbro, Denmark (d. 1981)
Boris Koutzen, Russian violinist, composer, and educator, born in Uman, Russia (now Ukraine) (d. 1966)
Johnny Farrell, American golfer (US Open 1928), born in White Plains, NY (d. 1988)
Whittaker Chambers, American writer (Time Magazine) and intelligence agent (defected Soviet spy), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1961)[1]
Laxmidas Purshottamdas Jai, Indian cricket batsman (1 Test; captain Bombay in inaugural Ranji Trophy win 1935), born in Bombay, India (d. 1968)
Maria Polydouri, Greek poet (Echo over chaos), born in Kalamata, Greece (d. 1930)
Juan Gil-Albert, Spanish poet and writer, born in Alcoy (d. 1994)
Clara Hale 'Mother Hale', American social activist (Hale House Center for disadvantaged children), born in Elizabeth Town, North Carolina (d. 1992)
Winfried Zillig, German opera composer, and conductor (Fantasia Irica), born in Würzburg, Germany (d. 1963)
Ned Glass, Polish-American actor (Sol-Julia, Uncle Moe-Bridget Loves Bernie), born in Poland (d. 1984)
Sigve Lie, Norwegian sailor (Olympic gold dragon class 1948, 52), born in Haugesund, Norway (d. 1958)
Walter Kaufmann, German-American conductor (Winnipeg Symphony, 1948-57), composer (Navaratnam), and musicologist (The Ragas of South India), born in Karlsbad, Germany (d. 1984)
(Edwin) "Eddy" Duchin, American jazz pianist and bandleader (Eddy Duchin Orchestra), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1951)
Abner Biberman, American director and actor (His Girl Friday, The Leopard Man), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1977)
Harry Carney, American tenor and baritone jazz saxophonist (Duke Ellington Orchestra, 1927-74), born in Boston, Massachussets (d. 1974)
Fauja Singh, Indian farmer often credited with being the world's oldest marathon runner, born in Beas Pind, British India (d. 2025)[1]
Günther Rennert, German film and opera director and administrator, born in Essen, Germany (d. 1978)
Joe Fortenberry, American basketball forward (captain Olympic gold 1936), born in Leo, Texas (d. 1993)
Carla van Neste, Belgian classical violinist (Trio Reine Elisabeth), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1992)
Lor Tok [Sawong Supsamruay], Thai comedian and actor (Ngern, Ngern, Ngern), born in Bangkok Yai, Thailand (d. 2002)
Art Lund, American pop baritone singer ("Mam'selle"), screen and stage actor (Fiorello!), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 1990)
Jeff Heath, Canadian baseball outfielder (MLB All Star 1941, 43, 45; Cleveland Indians), born in Fort William, Ontario (d. 1975)
Otto Wilhelm Fischer, Austrian actor (Helden, Ich suche dich), born in Klosterneuburg, Austria-Hungary (d. 2004)
Leon Janney (Ramon), American actor (Charly, Stolen Paradise, Hawk), born in Ogden, Utah (d. 1980)
Melville Shavelson, American film director, producer, screenwriter, and President of the Writers Guild of America West (1969-71; 79-81; 85-7), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2007)
Joseph Murray, American surgeon and pioneer of organ transplantation (Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1990), born in Milford, Massachusetts (d. 2012)[1][2]
Beau Jack, American boxer (NYSAC world lightweight champion 1942-44), born in Waynesboro, Georgia (d. 2000)
Douglas Allenbrook, American pianist, harpsichord player, and composer, born in Alton, Massachusetts (d. 2003)
Ken Reardon, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame defenseman and executive (Stanley Cup 1946; NHL All-Star 1946-50; Montreal Canadiens), born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2008)
Steve Race, British composer, jazz pianist and radio and television presenter (BBC's "My Music", 1967-94), born in Lincoln, England (d. 2009)
William Bergsma, American composer (The Fortunate Islands; The Murder of Comrade Sharik), and educator (Juilliard, 1946-63; University of Washington, 1963-86), born in Oakland, California (d. 1994)
(Irving) "Duke" Jordan, American jazz pianist (Charlie Parker, Stan Getz, Sonny Stitt), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2006)
Emil 'Bus' Mosbacher, American yachtsman (America's Cup 1962 [Weatherly], 1967 [Intrepid]), born in White Plains, New York (d. 1997)
Jimmie Lloyd [Logdon], American country and rockabilly singer ("I Got a Rocket in My Pocket"), born in Panther, Kentucky (d. 2001)
William Manchester, American historian (Death of a President), born in Attleboro, Massachusetts (d. 2004)
Robert "Bobby" Jordan, American actor (Dead End Kids, Kid Dynamite, Flying Wild), born in Harrison, New York (d. 1965)
Brendan Byrne, American politician (Governor of New Jersey 1974-82), born in West Orange, New Jersey (d. 2018)
Čkalja [Miodrag Petrović], Serbian actor and one of the most popular comedians of former Yugoslavia, born in Kruševac, Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (d. 2003)
Günther Becker, German classical and electro-acoustic composer, born in Forbach, Germany (d. 2007)
Kathy Stobart, British jazz tenor saxophonist, bandleader, and teacher, born in South Shields, England (d. 2014)
Sala Burton, American politician (Rep-D-CA, 1983-87), born in Białystok, Poland (d. 1987)
Charles Bressler, American tenor, specializing in early music (New York Pro Musica, 1953-63), and educator, born in Kingston, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Denis Lalanne, French sports journalist ('Le grand combat du XV de France'; 'Rugbymania: French flair attitude'), born in Pau, France (d. 2019)
Lyndon Brook, British actor (Invasion, Reach for the Sky), born in York, England (d. 2004)
Amos Milburn, American R&B singer and pianist ("One Scotch, One Bourbon, One Beer"), born in Houston, Texas (d. 1980)
Walter Bahr, American soccer midfielder (19 caps) and coach (Penn State), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2018)
Dimitri Frenkel Frank, Dutch writer and director (Hadimassa), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1988)
George Grizzard, American Emmy and Tony Award-winning actor (Wrong is Right, Bachelor Party, Attica), born in Roanoke Rapids, North Carolina (d. 2007)
